behaviors.description: The generated reply references specific points from the incoming email rather than producing a generic response.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is not about module wiring — it's about LLM output quality. LLM-DEP: Whether the reply actually references specific named entities, dates, or topics from the thread is entirely determined by what the real LLM produces. A stub always returns whatever you hard-code, so it trivially 'references specific points' without testing anything real. REAL-INFRA: No OS/subprocess needed. REJECTION-GATE: Could be demoted to Integration only if we were testing prompt construction (did we include the thread text?), but the behavior explicitly targets the *reply content*, not the prompt pipeline — so Integration is too low.
behaviors.test_description: Send a real email thread containing specific named entities, figures, and requests (e.g., a project deadline, a person's name, a dollar amount) to the draft generation endpoint with a real LLM. Assert that the returned draft mentions at least two of those specific details, rather than producing a boilerplate reply.

behaviors.description: The factual consistency check flags claims in the draft that contradict entries in the knowledge base.
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: Detecting semantic contradiction between a free-text draft claim and a KB entry requires real semantic understanding. A rule-based or stub checker would only catch exact-string matches; real contradictions (paraphrase, negation, unit mismatch) only surface with a real model. MOCK-HIDE: A stub consistency checker trivially passes any test — it returns whatever flag you configure. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network needed. REJECTION-GATE: Demoting to Integration would only be valid if the consistency check were purely deterministic (e.g., regex matching), but the behavior says 'factual consistency' which implies semantic reasoning; there is no behavior text that limits the checker to deterministic logic.
behaviors.test_description: Seed the knowledge base with a concrete factual entry (e.g., 'Product X ships in 3 business days'). Feed the consistency checker a draft that contradicts it (e.g., 'as you know, Product X ships same-day') using the real LLM/model-backed checker. Assert that the checker returns at least one flagged inconsistency referencing that claim.

behaviors.description: When the knowledge base has no relevant entries, the draft includes a disclaimer rather than fabricating facts.
behaviors.justification: LLM-DEP: 'Includes a disclaimer rather than fabricating facts' describes the *content* of what the LLM generates when signaled that the KB is empty. A stub always returns a pre-written response, so it cannot reveal whether the real model hallucinates. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the LLM always passes — the disclaimer is baked into the stub. REAL-INFRA: None required. REJECTION-GATE: One could argue Integration tests 'does the system pass the empty-KB signal to the LLM?' but the behavior's stated outcome is the draft's content (disclaimer vs. fabrication), not prompt construction — so Integration is insufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Configure the KB to return zero results for any query. Invoke the draft generation step with a real LLM on an email thread that asks for specific factual information. Assert that the returned draft contains an explicit uncertainty/disclaimer phrase (e.g., 'I don't have enough information') and does not assert any specific fact that was absent from the email thread itself.

behaviors.description: The approval workflow blocks sending until the user explicitly approves — no draft is sent automatically.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is at the boundary between the draft-presentation module and the email-sending module — it appears when the two are wired together but the gate is bypassed. REAL-INFRA: Real SMTP is not needed; the failure (premature send call) is caught by verifying the send function was/was not invoked, which an in-process mock captures perfectly. LLM-DEP: No — this is deterministic control flow; whether the gate fires is independent of LLM output. MULTI-STEP: No chained agentic steps. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) No real OS/network needed. (b) No LLM dependency. (c) No multi-agentic chain. Integration confirmed.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the draft-presentation module to a mocked email-send function. Trigger the full approval workflow with a completed draft but without submitting an approval signal. Assert the mock send function was never called. Then submit an explicit approval event and assert the mock send function is called exactly once with the correct draft payload.

behaviors.description: The full sequence — read thread, generate draft, check facts, present for approval — works end-to-end for a real email thread.
behaviors.justification: MULTI-STEP: The behavior explicitly names four sequential pipeline stages: (1) read thread, (2) generate draft (LLM), (3) check facts (LLM/model), (4) present for approval. Steps 2 and 3 each involve real model calls, making this at minimum two chained agentic steps. LLM-DEP: Both generation and consistency checking depend on real model output; failures at either step (or in handoff between steps) are invisible with stubs. REAL-INFRA: Reading a 'real email thread' may involve real I/O, reinforcing the need for end-to-end execution. REJECTION-GATE: Agentic would be insufficient because the behavior explicitly requires all four stages to execute in sequence — an Agentic test covering only draft generation misses the integration of fact-checking and approval handoff.
behaviors.test_description: Provide a real (or realistic fixture-sourced) email thread containing verifiable factual claims. Execute the complete pipeline end-to-end with real LLM calls for both generation and consistency checking. Assert that: the draft is non-empty and references the thread; the fact-check step runs and produces a result (pass or flagged); and the draft is held in a pending-approval state — not sent — at pipeline completion.
